stimulus: Many veteran translators will tell you that judging the quality of a literary translation depends not only on accuracy and fluency but also on intuitions about voice and register developed over years of practice. In a recent study, however, researchers trained a large language model on thousands of paired source texts and professional translations and found that the model could rank candidate translations roughly as well as a panel of expert translators. Some translators have voiced concern that they might be replaced by such models, but the researchers contend that this outcome is highly unlikely.
stem: Which finding, if true, would most directly support the researchers’ contention?

answerOptions.content: A survey of literary translators indicates that ranking candidate translations is only a small fraction of their professional work, most of which involves creative tasks the model cannot perform.
In the researchers’ study, the model could rank translations far faster than the human panel could.
In the researchers’ study, neither the model nor the panel could correctly rank every translation pair.
A survey of literary translators indicates that few translators have formal training specifically in evaluating translation quality.

rationale: Choice A is the best answer because the researchers’ contention is that translators are unlikely to be replaced. If ranking is a small part of a translator’s work and most of the job involves creative tasks the model cannot do, then matching the model on ranking does not threaten the profession. Choice B (speed advantage) actually supports the replacement worry. Choice C says both have limits but doesn’t distinguish their roles. Choice D speaks to training, not to whether ranking exhausts translation work.
